does: This paper presents Qwen-Image-2.0-RL, a post-training pipeline that enhances a diffusion model through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and on-policy distillation (OPD). The core contribution involves constructing task-specific composite reward models by fine-tuning vision-language models with pointwise scoring and chain-of-thought reasoning, covering alignment, aesthetics, and portrait fidelity for text-to-image generation, plus instruction-following accuracy and face identity preservation for image editing. The authors develop a scalable GRPO-based RL training framework incorporating hybrid classifier-free guidance to preserve pre-trained knowledge, prompt curation via reward range filtering, and per-category reward weight calibration. A final on-policy distillation stage merges task-specialized RL policies across both text-to-image and editing capabilities, demonstrating improved visual quality and instruction-following performance.
connects: This work builds on the established paradigm of applying RLHF to diffusion models, extending recent advances in reward modeling for image generation beyond simple metrics toward structured, multi-dimensional evaluation. It connects to prior research on vision-language model-based reward systems and chain-of-thought reasoning for scoring, while incorporating insights from recent RL techniques like GRPO. The paper responds to the challenge of training diffusion models to satisfy multiple objectives simultaneously—aesthetic quality, semantic alignment, and task-specific constraints—by developing composite reward architectures. It also engages with the technical problem of preserving pre-trained knowledge during RL fine-tuning through hybrid guidance strategies, addressing a known tension in applying RL to large generative models.
enables: This work enables more controllable and higher-quality image generation by demonstrating how multi-objective reward modeling and careful RL training can improve both text-to-image and image editing capabilities. The approach of task-specific reward models and on-policy distillation for merging specialized policies could be extended to other multimodal generation tasks or domains requiring diverse quality criteria. The framework opens directions for research into more sophisticated reward aggregation mechanisms, scalable preference learning from diverse human annotators, and techniques for balancing competing objectives in generative model training. The demonstration of preserving pre-trained knowledge during RL fine-tuning provides foundations for applying similar techniques to other large diffusion or autoregressive models where maintaining general capabilities during task-specific improvement is critical.
